---
title: "npRmpi is back from the grave"
subtitle: "A draft note on the forthcoming np/npRmpi 0.70-2 release"
author: "Jeffrey S. Racine"
date: "2026-05-11"
draft: true
feed: false
image: "images/preview.svg"
image-alt: "Abstract release graphic connecting np, npRmpi, crs, CRAN, and the gallery"
categories:
  - Research
  - Computing
  - Open Web
editor:
  markdown:
    wrap: 72
---

File this one under "things I did not quite expect to be writing about
in 2026".

Some background is likely in order. The `np` package has been on CRAN
for a very long time now, and has quietly accumulated the joys and
sorrows that come with long-lived research software. It is used in
courses, papers, replications, and by people I have never met who are
trying to estimate something flexible without pretending that every
object in sight is Gaussian, linear, or continuous. That remains the
point of the package.

The upcoming `np` 0.70-2 release is therefore not a new package dressed
up with a new version number. It is a fairly substantial modernization
of a package whose core purpose remains unchanged: nonparametric kernel
methods for regression, density, distribution, quantile, partially
linear, single-index, and smooth-coefficient models, with mixed data
types treated as first-class citizens rather than as an afterthought.

The slightly more surprising news is that `npRmpi` is also back. The
package had effectively gone dormant after its
[2014 CRAN archive entry](https://cran.r-project.org/src/contrib/Archive/npRmpi/),
then received the small 0.60-20 update needed to get it listed on CRAN
again. The goal now is to have `npRmpi` move in step with `np` for users
whose problems are large enough that parallel bandwidth selection,
bootstrap inference, or plot-data construction are worth spreading over
MPI workers. In other words, `npRmpi` is not a different statistical
story. It is the same story with more machinery available when the job
calls for it.

There is a third package in this release train as well. The refreshed
`crs` package supplies the current NOMAD solver interface used heavily
by `np` and `npRmpi`, and the old `crs::npglpreg()` surface has been
retired in favour of the more natural

```r
npreg(..., nomad = TRUE)
```

interface in `np` and `npRmpi`. This is the sort of change that is not
especially glamorous, but it matters. Users should not have to remember
which package temporarily housed which optimization route. They should
be able to ask the estimator for the model they want, select NOMAD when
appropriate, and get on with the analysis.

The visible usability changes are mostly in that spirit. Plotting has a
cleaner public interface, so common requests now look like ordinary R
arguments rather than a tour through historical option names. Bootstrap
bands, asymptotic standard errors, plot behavior, renderer choice, grid
controls, and perspective controls are now much easier to express, while
the older `plot.errors.*` names remain accepted for compatibility. The
goal was to make the common things readable without breaking old code
that people may reasonably still have in a replication archive.

There are also a number of repairs and extensions under the surface.
Conditional quantile regression has improved support for gradients and
standard errors. Partially linear regression prediction can now return
standard errors. Several local-linear and adaptive-nearest-neighbor
routes have been tightened so that selected degree and basis choices are
honoured consistently. Discrete probability estimates are projected back
onto a proper probability simplex where appropriate. Bandwidth-search
and plot-bootstrap routes now fail earlier and more informatively when
they should, and are less inclined to sit silently while they do useful
work.

For `npRmpi`, the corresponding work was not simply a matter of turning
on MPI and hoping for the best. The resurrected package needed renewed
attention to worker setup, OpenMPI runtime discovery, master/worker
dispatch, object-fed plot helpers, bootstrap fanout, and keeping the
parallel package genuinely independent at runtime. The intended result
is simple to state: the serial and MPI packages should agree in
statistical behaviour, while the MPI version should give users a
practical path for computationally heavier work.

All of this has also prompted a broader cleanup of the public-facing
material around the packages. The package gallery has been refreshed so
that examples, documentation, vignettes, and issue links are easier to
find. My research and CV pages have also been updated so that software,
papers, books, and replication material are less scattered across the
web. This may sound like housekeeping, but for open research software it
is not a minor detail. If people cannot find the work, understand what
is current, or report a problem when they find one, the software is less
useful than it ought to be.

Once the final tarballs have cleared CRAN, the links below should be the
first places to look. Before this becomes the R-announce note, the
version numbers should be checked one last time against CRAN; the
intended release train is `np` 0.70-2, `npRmpi` 0.70-2, and `crs`
0.15-43.

- [`np` on CRAN](https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/np/index.html)
- [`npRmpi` on CRAN](https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/npRmpi/index.html)
- [`crs` on CRAN](https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/crs/index.html)
- [The package gallery](https://jeffreyracine.github.io/gallery/)
- [Research contributions](https://jeffreyracine.github.io/research/)
- [Curriculum vitae](https://jeffreyracine.github.io/vitae/)
- [`np`/`npRmpi` source and issues](https://github.com/JeffreyRacine/R-Package-np)
- [`crs` source and issues](https://github.com/JeffreyRacine/R-Package-crs)

If you are coming to the packages after a long absence, the main thing
to try is probably the simplest:

```r
library(np)

fit <- npreg(y ~ x1 + x2, data = dat, nomad = TRUE)

plot(fit, errors = "bootstrap", bootstrap = "inid", B = 399)
```

For larger jobs, the corresponding `npRmpi` workflow is intended to feel
familiar rather than exotic. Start the workers, ask for the same
statistical object, and let the package spread the computationally
expensive parts where it can.

This post is still a draft because CRAN has the last word on timing,
and because announcement prose should never get too far ahead of the
actual packages sitting on the CRAN mirrors. But the milestone is close
enough that it seems worth saying out loud: `np` has received a serious
usability pass, `crs` has been simplified around its current role, and
`npRmpi` is no longer merely a line in the archive.

Color me happy.
